## 📈 Scaling & Growth

*1011 insights*

- It's connected to your tools, your processes, and it understands your business.
  — *agencymavericks: Your Next Employee Is AI (And No, You Don't Need to Fire Your Team)*

- Over 3 years, that's nearly 3 quarters of a million dollars in wages before you add in super or 401K or whatever you call it where you live, then there's sick leave, holidays, training, recruitment, management.
  — *agencymavericks: Your Next Employee Is AI (And No, You Don't Need to Fire Your Team)*

- Because we know that a percentage of people that we talk to will want to hire us for our done-for-you service, where we install and train the AI employee for them.
  — *agencymavericks: Your Next Employee Is AI (And No, You Don't Need to Fire Your Team)*

smattering of this Exquisite cheese on our signature margarita true story go to the cheese wanker.com to fact check it and they offer these insights to their customers for free in the hope that products these include a cookbook brimming with tantalizing recipes a line of signature ingredients in kitchen tools and even her own cooking show she's realized that her true worth lies not only in the act of cooking but also in the profound insights and creativity that she contributes to The Culinary world now a good Chef in Australia gets paid about 80 grand a year let's call it a hundred [ __ ] it let's call it 120 Grand a year Jamie Oliver's net worth is over 200 million dollars most web design and digital agencies resemble the first chef as they freely give away their most valuable insights in the hope of attracting clients who will then hire them for their standard range of services such as SEO ad management web design copywriting and everything else that's rapidly become a commodity however the True Value lies in the agency's ability to think strategically and offer unique insights that can help a client use the interwebs to achieve their business goals in other words round of the 12-week body transformation program the 40 year old mum was 115 kilos depressed smoked drank over ate and had no social life in her own words she was pretty much at the end of the road fast forward three years and Hoffman lost 43 kilos became a personal trainer and broke the world Burpee record doing 40 burpees in a minute and 1500 in an hour what the actual [ __ ] she still signs up to the 12-week body transformation every time it launches and has not missed around the 12-week body transformation is a program designed and presented by Michelle Bridges who started out working in a gym in Sydney not satisfied with the impact she was having by just working in a gym she decided to stalk a local TV host and Pitch the idea of a fitness segment on a popular daytime TV show here in Australia eventually the TV host organized a meeting with the producers just to shut her up that Fitness segment went on to become its own TV show called The Biggest Loser and Michelle Bridges was catapulted to fame as the No Holds Barred no [ __ ] trainer on the show who helped contestants win by losing the most weight Michelle became so overwhelmed with requests for diet and fitness advice that she decided to package up her knowledge into a product actually several products cookbooks Fitness DVDs and eventually once the Australian internet caught up with the rest of the world and allowed for Reliable video streaming she put up an online membership program called the 12-week body transformation which contributed to her generating over 60 million dollars in Revenue back in 2018.

day and said am I a better version of me if I look at myself compared to a week ago a month ago last year can I say honestly to another person that I am better today if I continue to compare myself to - I spent 168 hours with Richard Branson and here's what I learned a few years ago I got an invite to spend a week with Richard Branson at his home in Switzerland I had impostor syndrome out the ying-yang but I went and I said to myself I'm just going to go with one specific reason I want to see how the billionaire that every other billionaire wants to be like how does he operate in his day-to-day what does he actually like how does he hire people what are the kind of people that he hangs out with and I watched him get up eat breakfast with his assistant and and leave for the rest of the day just to play he spent most of his time with us not doing any work not being distracted not being stressed out but just having fun and living life and over a few days I asked his assistant how do you work with Richard and that's when she shared with me the simple process that's changed my life forever the first thing I learned is he doesn't touch technology every morning when they have breakfast that is when she writes down all the things that she doesn't know how to deal with and presents them to Richard and he gives her feedback and ideas and suggestions and then she takes all of that and all the other requests that come in throughout the day and she moves those things forward while Richard is hanging out with us so that way he can be so present in his dayto day he can hear for opportunities he's listening he's in the moment and it's a fascinating thing to see because when you live in a world where everybody's distracted here's a guy that's worth billions that is only there for the conversation so he wrotes 100% of his conversations through his assistant all the demands all the requests all the needs on his time they go through one person and her name is Helen even all his emails they go through Helen he doesn't read them for the most part he walks around with a legal pad and he writes down ideas and notes and I think he just rips them apart and puts them in his pocket and when he gets a chance he'll sit down and review his ideas with Helen maybe over breakfast and that's the degree of technology that he's got in his life he keeps it so simple it's not complicated he doesn't have his login to his CRM or his email or to his bank accounts he doesn't doesn't need that he's a connector and idea guy and I think a lot of us would benefit from learning how to do that better the second thing I learned is he licenses his name when people see the Virgin logo everywhere like virgin record Virgin Media Virgin Mobile Virgin Airline they think like Richard owns all those companies but the truth is out of 400 companies more than half Richard only owns a minority percentage of the businesses or it's a licensing deal companies pay a licensing fee to use the name the Virgin group of businesses is a $16.6 billion Revenue business as of I believe 2019 with over 71,000 employees but many of those businesses they only own a minority stake in them or their licensing deal where they've been paid to use the Virgin brand I remember one night we were having dinner and I asked Richard a simple question I said in business what is the most important thing that you've learned I was like thinking it's going to be some deep secret that nobody else has ever heard before and he says to me brand the power of brand and I'll be honest with you at the time I didn't really get it cuz I'm a software guy so I was thinking there's got to be more to this what does he mean by the power of brand and then I realized that most of the companies that he's involved in he's licensed his brand to other businesses and they pay him for the Virgin name to be associated with the Virgin brand that is a powerful idea where you can build a business that has such demand like the Nike check mark where you get paid to use that logo to create the essence of what that means the cool part is I realized you know it took me a few years that the most powerful brand that we all have is our name it's who we are it's what we're about it's it's our reach and our reputation and Richard just made me realize that had I been more deliberate back when I met him and continued to invest my personal brand many of the dreams many of the goals I've been out to accomplish would have happened a lot quicker the third thing I learned is that he monetizes his passions see most people don't realize that wealthy people find ways to take essentially an expense and turn it into an asset to take something that costs money and turn it into a revenue generating item he does this with everything I mean the property we're staying in it's part of the Virgin limited edition boutique hotels most of his homes from neker Island to his property in Africa every one of them there are places that you can also rent for hundreds of thousands of dollars per week but that's just one example I mean everything from space travel with Galactic to music with Virgin Records to kiteboarding is hosted an event called my taii you know it's kind of the modern-day golfing for Silicon Valley I have friends that instead of putting their money in a bank account where it earns interest they literally figured out how to hack cars purchase these super cars they get allocations they buy right and over time as an asset it makes some money same thing with watches if you know what you're doing you buy at the right time these things are investments that will hold their value and go up over time wealthy people do this with art for tax reasons see the rich know how how to turn a passion from an expense into a revenue generating item the fourth thing I learned is he's a master connector a big reason that he has all these properties is because he likes to host events and meet other people I mean even on the trip I was on there was probably 20 other entrepreneurs from him Ferris to Brian Johnson the biohacker and amongst the group he ended up investing in three or four other projects through his Venture Capital fund so I would say almost 70% of Branson's time isn't spend running companies but meeting and connecting with entrepreneurs once he'll invest in collaborate with maybe start new companies with maybe license his brand to these are the types of things that leverage his zone of genius and I was really inspired by it I mean I ended up starting hosting more dinners organizing events doing things like my ski trip with 48 entrepreneurs to snowbiking trips to mountain bike trips like I just realized like why not integrate everything into one it just makes - to go to sleep I've been sleeping for 7 hours which brings us to Tiny habit number 11 invest in your environment 4 years ago I was rening a house and I was there for 6 months and the office had this beautiful window but it was directly in the sunlight where I couldn't see anything and I decided within the first few days to hire a sign company to come put a film over the glass and when my wife heard about it she thought that's crazy why why would you spend 1,200 bucks to get the glass covered so that you can stay in that spot and I was like I don't want to move my desk I like the energy of this space this environment and I'm willing to spend $11,200 so I can be in the energy of the vibe and it's funny I see people make this mistake all the time they spend most of their time at their desk and they don't have a great chair they don't have great monitors they have invested in keyboards they have invested in the tools maybe it's the camera maybe it's the ring light whatever would make your work environment feel fun is something I'm a big fan of investing in which brings us to Tiny habit number 12 follow one course until successful that's what Focus stands for see the richest people in the world do one thing they just do it for 20 years they go all in and dedicate themselves to a decade they don't get distracted they don't have three businesses with one making some profit and the other two being unprofitable they don't diversify earlier on because diversification is diversification if you want to create wealth you have to be concentrated so don't be easily distracted pick something and decide to be a master at it which brings us to Tiny habit number 13 protect your space words matter what you hear matters I'm a big fan of turning off the news cutting out toxic friends curating my feed to show me opportunity and possibility the end of the day the quality of your inputs will determine the quality of your outputs you have to protect yourself from the things you're being exposed to which brings us to Tiny habit number 14 stop asking for money there's this great Pitbull song song that says ask for money get advice ask for advice get money twice and that to me is How I Live my whole life most people are asking for money from investors and friends and family why don't you ask for advice and what happens is when you do that the person tells you all the things that would need to be true for them to be excited about it and you just get really good at agreeing with them and next thing you know instead of just getting advice you'll actually get money which brings us a tiny habit number 15 live below your means being wealthy isn't a number it's actually a ratio when I was making 120k a year at 25 I was driving a 15-year-old car I was living off of 30 to 40% of my income today I probably live off of just over 10% of my income why because it's not what you make it's what you spend broke people buy stuff rich people buy things that make them money if you want to be wealthy don't buy things that you don't need to impress people you don't like which brings us to Tiny habit number 16 give back to your community I learned a long time ago at 17 in rehab that I can only keep what I give away if I want sobriety I have to help other people be sober if I want to be rich I got to help other people be rich if you want abs guess what go help other people get abs see when you have that abundance mentality focus on other people and being of service you will create the opportunities in your life to get those results most people are running around with scarcity you want to be there with expansion so share your things if you have a nice house invite people into your home if you have nice cars please share your freaking cars I have a neighbor that's got a beautiful car that never sees the light of day cuz he's worried he's going to curb his rims or somebody's going to lean up against it and that thing has not brought anybody else Joy except for him from a selfish point of view you want to be the person that brings Smiles on other people's faces every day which brings us to Tiny habit number 17 reflect on your achievements often times when I'm feeling doubt or I'm not certain about my decisions or I've got a big meeting a big keynote I got to give and I'm just not feeling it I go back to a thing called my achievement list and this is a list of all the accomplishments I've had over the years no matter how small from my very first kickflip to my first exit I've listed them and I review them because the energy of those moments reminds me what I've accomplished in the past what I'm capable of doing and when I'm sitting in this low energy self Deus mindset I want to go back to those moments where I've won because massive success is built on tiny little wins and that's how we build momentum when you're in momentum you can't be stopped the hardest part about winning is getting back into momentum if you're not feeling it you need to use your achievement list to get that energy going it's like a rocket when it takes off it uses 90% of all the fuel to just get out into orbit once it's in orbit it can maintain your achievement list can help you stay in that momentum so you don't ever come back down to earth and you stay flying high which brings us to Tiny habit number 18 be blissfully dissatisfied I got this language from my coach Ed milet Because he believes that you can achieve and have a lot and be grateful for that stuff but in the same moment be dissatisfied with the fact that you know you're here to do more I think about this all the time when people are like how much money is enough and I'm like what are you talking about as long as I'm here I'm breathing and my heart is into the work to create for other people I'm going to wake up I'm going to create I might have un attachment to the result but I'm definitely involved in the creation I just believe if you're listen to this that you're here to do something more with your life that you're here to do something massive with your life that you're here to create something special and it doesn't matter how big that is if you're getting up every day you might as well go build something so if you want to learn the seven skills of the top 1% click the video and I'll see you on the other side

- these are the four CEO skills that you need to get to 10 million a year number one is value and this is how we grow this is at the base of every great company 0 to 300K is about finding a huge pain in the market and collecting resources to solve it I first learned this strategy reading the book the four steps to the Epiphany by an incredible entreer named Steve blank in that book he goes over the four steps that you use to pre-sell your product before you even build it and I know that sounds crazy I actually used these four steps recently in launching my new AI product before we even went to Market whether it's a consulting company a Service Company honestly a coaching company whatever it is make an offer but see most people mess it up because they're just trying to do a sale what they don't get is how to validate their idea with the market so here's how we do it right the first thing we have to get right is Discovery okay and Discovery is all about talking to the market we want to talk to real people we want to get outside of our office not hide behind our keyboard and pick up the phone or go into the world and talk to people that have the problem that we think we can solve we want to ask them questions about the challenges they face around the solution that we want to offer we want to figure out how they make decisions about solving these problem we want to understand do we have a painkiller or do we have a vitamin is this something that's meaningful for them to want to solve cuz if not you can spend all your time in your office behind your keyboard trying to solve a problem when you finally go out there to sell it nobody wants to buy the second step is validation validation once you have an idea of how your Market wants to buy that they have a problem then you want to go collect data you want to understand how big the market is you want to figure out what the one clear problem is you want to see how people have sold to that market before what's the chance Channel they've used what's the language what's the offer what's the ads you can do a lot of work on the validation step before you ever build anything so that you don't waste your time the third step is to test okay and the way we test is we build a clickable prototype we simulate the offer some people call it a Meo a minimum economical viable offer I like to create clickable prototypes when I'm building software and I want to show it to real buyers I wanted to show it to people that have the problem and through that process I get feedback actually on simulated software or on a solution an offer specifically where they can evaluate it and then ask me questions doing it that way ensures that we don't just get a bunch of people to say nice things and tell us hey this is a great idea go off and build it and then we wake up one day and realize nobody wanted the thing we built the fourth step is offer here's why this one's so important what we we want to do in that step is get money get dollars get them to pull out their wallet give you a credit card pay with real money I can't tell you the amount of times that I've had people say to my face great idea amazing keep building it I would love to be a customer and then I spend all the time I hire the engineers I build a prototype or I build a team I build the whole thing and when I go back to these folks it said it was a great idea they go oh I'm busy send me the the link I'll sign up and they never use the thing that I've built we want to get real dollars so that people validate with their money not with their words so if potential because it is huge it's bigger than you can imagine I believe that in my soul because I believe that for every person so start with the for inventory the second thing to amplify your environment is your beliefs and the way to pump up your beliefs is through books I want to share with you four specific books that will allow you to build that net Network build that environment build those relationships that are going to take your personal Network your room and amplify it so that you have those people in your life now that you created some space do it in for inventory number one is called Mastermind dinners by my good friend Jason gaynard super fun story when he was considering writing the book I actually had written this ebook about hosting Founders dinners because him and I actually met through you know hosting people for meals eventually spoke at his event Mastermind talks and I was just so passionate about him because he was doing dinners on another level literally 25 people custom menus all this stuff curating facilitating he even had question cards and prompts at the table he took things to another level so he wrote a book and it's called Mastermind dinners and it is one of my favorite books on the topic so if you want to be inspired and overcome some beliefs to get you to host your first dinner with let's just start with three other people so you would make four you want to read that book the second most important book is Never Eat Alone by Keith ferrazzi I've read it like probably 15 years ago and not only has Keith become somebody I consider a friend I actually went and spent time with him and saw him execute the stuff in his book he literally talks about all these different strategies he does with like two cell phones and inviting people to work out literally that was what happened I was like Hey I'm going to La I love to meet in person invited me for a workout then we went to brunch afterwards at his favorite spot and then he invited other people to the brunch for me to meet them to to kind of like increase my experience but also for them to meet me and me to meet that it's just crazy how he did that so highly recommend Never Eat Alone the third one is the Bible for all of this it's How to Win Friends and Influence People by Dale Carnegie and I have this funny story about the book because one day I had a team member coming over to my house right before she was flying out and she had her luggage her carry-on and then this uh binder because she was taking this you know uh Dale Carnegie course and it's all about like you know going to events and remember people's names right like the big part of like asking somebody's name and remember it and try to you know different techniques and strategies and my father-in-law Caesar looks at the the chorusing and just says to her he goes ah that that that training I took that a long time ago never forgot a person's name that that Dave Carnegie that's great stuff and she kind of they do they built a very lean version of their affiliate program which was hey if you refer a the Dropbox will give you some free storage we will'll actually increase your storage for free if you how we can build a path to that so it was ultimately something we needed to charge for and really I suppose on the other side of that it's something that's so highly valuable for the client at the end so for us the paid Discovery is kind of a mixture of two things it's uh that that chat about the business finding out you know why are we having a discussion in the first place and and ultimately what that's going to help them get from A to B and realize that without you they can't do it themselves sales is is not hard as I said it is a process so hiring or training team members to do sales is one of the most leveraged things you can do in your agency because it gets you completely out of the business and someone else can generate revenue on board clients and then your team can deliver and ultimately you want to build a self-managing sales team you want to build a sales team of even if it's one person who reports to you and tells you what's going on with prospects and leads and sales calls so that you can further refine your ideal client you can further refine your messaging you can pivot your marketing you can choose a different channel for Content marketing based on what your onboarded into the agency you don't get overwhelmed Med and start dropping the ball I can promise you that a client services business is one of the most difficult business models to scale because it requires you to do different things for every client that walks through the door and I just want to help you fix that right now so that you can actually scale there are two categories of products as far as I'm concerned one we call a fire starter and the other we call an accelerator a fire starter is designed to highlight a problem to a client it doesn't solve the problem it just shows the client the work that needs to be done it starts a fire in their business or it shows them hey there's a fire over there and we need to deal with that an accelerator puts out the fire and accelerates the growth or the results or the outcome for one part of the client's business it's not the sum total of everything you do it just helps them solve one problem I'll give you some examples in a moment your signature system is the sum total of all of your fire starters and all of your accelerators put together and packaged up into a 12-month road map and delivered with excellent customer care and Reporting and communication and I'll talk a little bit more about that in a moment paid Discovery is an example of a fire starter you're run a paid Discovery workshop with a client and you do it correctly you won't solve any problems you just collect a whole bunch of really good data from them and then you go back to them and show them all of the things that are broken and not working and that can be fixed and you'll make your recommendations on what they should do next but at the end of PID Discovery nothing is fixed we just have have an audit if you like on all of the things that need attention and the order in which we're going to fix them now that hopefully you've sold a paid Discovery session your job when it comes to starting to productize your services is to document how you deliver that paid Discovery Workshop so that you can start to hand it over to a team member in the future and I would also suggest that you put together a tear sheet for paid Discovery and A Tear sheet is a simple one pager that you can leave behind with a client or email as a PDF and it outlines exactly what's involved what's included in paid Discovery what they get the investment sets all the expectations so that they know exactly what they're buying one of the reasons I really like tear sheets is because it forces you to do the deep thinking required to understand exactly what paid Discovery is and how you can teach another team member to do that you'll know this is working when you've sold your first paid Discovery Workshop when you've gone back and delivered your first recommendation session and you've upgraded that CL client from Paid Discovery into a growth plan or an ongoing retainer just one note we don't call them retainers because in our experience nobody wants to pay for a retainer we also don't call PID Discovery paid Discovery because nobody wants to pay for Discovery so we call paid Discovery a digital road map and we call our retainers growth plans seems to be that clients are happy to pay for a digital road map and they're happy to pay for a growth plan but as I said they don't want to pay for Discovery or a retainer so just change the language the advanced tactics here around paid Discovery is to make sure that your paid process is completely documented that your team are delivering paid Discovery and that they are converting paid Discovery into growth plans at a minimum of 70% without you doing anything we had a conversation the other day with one of our agencies Simon chin from Flow State and he said they're at a 95% conversion rate from pay Discovery into growth plan and he is not involved at all his team are doing everything and that is a massive win for him and for us and so that's what you should be aiming for that really is the dream where your team are talking to clients running PID Discovery workshops and converting them into growth plans and as I said this is an advanced tactic so start off slowly start to fix problems for clients and how do we turn those Services into products so I have a device that I use called a growth blueprint and I recommend it to all of our agencies a growth blueprint is a two-page document that is a visual flowchart of how a service works and I'll give you an example in a moment and then on the second page is a checklist of all the things that need to happen to make that a reality this deliberately does not live in clickup or teamwork or ass or Monday or whatever project management system you use I build mine in canva or whimsical and there you can draw them on a napkin or a whiteboard the more analog the better don't put these into your project management systems and the way way that I use these is I show the flowchart to the client so let's talk about a lead gen blueprint for a client if we're going to generate leads off your website here's what happens and I'll literally walk them through a flowchart to say we've got traffic coming here to this landing page there's a call to action on the landing page they click the button a popup appears here's a form they enter their details they go into your email list we deliver an email with the free resource and then the sales team can follow them up I just draw that at a very high level and I get the client to agree yes that's exactly what we want and then on page two of that document is a checklist of all the things that need to happen to make that a reality so we need to identify the top performing web page on their website that gets most of the traffic we need to build a lead magnet that is relevant we need to install a popup manager and a form we need to plug that form into their CRM of course we do most of this in high level which makes it super easy but the technology doesn't matter then we need to deliver the email with the lead magnet then we need to alert the sales team to pick up the phone and make a call we just have a checklist of all the things that need to happen once the client approves the flowchart the checklist goes to the team and then the team gets the thing done in whatever project management system they use as an agency owner I actually don't care whether they use a or notion or teamwork or Monday or clickup not interested they can do it in slack if they like as long as the job gets done and they're efficient I don't care at some point my ops manager might care but as an agency owner I don't care what they use I just want the team to be happy and to be efficient and not frustrated so my job as an agency owner is to clear the obstacles so that the team can do their job not put more obstacles in their way by forcing them to use a system that I like but they might not like it's up to them how they want to manage their to-do list so the growth blueprint is deliberately away from the project management system and Visually allows the client to approve what it is they're buying and then has a checklist to tell the team what needs to happen and you can have growth blueprints for webinar registrations e-commerce cart abandonment Black Friday promotions email marketing campaigns ad management social media calendars you can have growth blueprints for any service that you deliver in your agency and that's how you turn your services into products so my suggestion to you is to choose one service probably the most common service that you deliver map it out as a flowchart and ask yourself how do we do this 80% of the time you know the 20% of outlier clients that come in that make us jump through hoops and are complete pain in their ass and do things differently and we have to reinvent stuff Let's ignore them let's just ask ourselves how do we do this 80% of the time and document that in a flowchart and a checklist and congratulations you've just productized your first service once you've done that develop a tear sheet for that product talk about what's included talk about what's not included talk about the scope talk about the benefits talk about how it's delivered talk about the transformation that it's going to make for your client talk about investment time frames think of it as a product that a client can buy off the shelf it's the same every time it's the same price every time it's the same deliverable it takes the same length of time we do it all in this Tech stack if they come in and they have some weird Erp that we need to integrate with well guess what that's out of scope that's a whole different project maybe we don't want to do that anymore maybe everything we do is just done in a high level or active campaign or whatever system we're using document the delivery of the product and then write up a tear sheet for it and watch your bespoke services that are all over the place get really tight really fast the intermediate tactics here are to make sure that multiple products are documented in your agency I would just pick the top five that you sell 80% of the time we sell this and 80% breaks make sure that your automations are reviewed at least every 90 days we're moving all of our documentation over to notion really for one main reason is that when we document an sop in notion we can set a reminder to have that sop reviewed by the author every 90 days so they get a notification where they have to go and review the SOP and make sure it's still relevant the other thing I would suggest from the get- go to make sure that your team take ownership of at least half of the automations in the agency otherwise as I said it's all going to come down to what you know in your head and when it comes to scaling your agency so I'm just going to run through the things that you should do in order to make sure that you recruit hire and manage a team of a players the very first exercise is to run through your or chart as it is today and run through your org chart as it needs to be in 12 months time and identify any gaps that you've got currently and any gaps that you will have over the next 12 months this is a boring exercise but it is absolutely essential otherwise you don't know who to recruit next biggest mistake I've seen agencies make and I've made myself is hiring someone based on their availability not on their ability and also moving people sideways in an agency when they're not working out in their role because well you know they're nice and we want them to hang around and we don't have the kahunas to fire them don't do that make sure that everyone on the team is fulfilling a role and making a meaningful contribution to the company the only way to do that is to understand your org chart what does it look like now and what does it need to look like in 12 months time the second Super valuable asset that we've dialed in in our business here and we help agencies with is job scorecards for each role on that org chart and a job scorecard very simply the difference between a job scorecard and a job description is a job description outlines the tasks that someone needs to do as part of their job whereas a job scorecard outlines the outcomes that they are responsible for I don't don't particularly care how they get there as an agency owner I don't care what tasks people have got on their daily to-do list what I care about is outcomes I care that the thing gets done that the outcome is achieved and I know that people like autonomy and they like to find their own way there so I give my team the freedom to figure out how to do it and if they need help they can ask me of course that's what I'm here for but I'm not here to tell them how to do their job I'm not very good at doing their job anyway which is why I'm a business owner not a business operator so job scorecard cards outline the outcomes that a role is responsible for not the tasks they are supposed to perform once you start to build a team of a players it's important that you keep in touch and for God's sake I hate meetings as much as the next person but there are some meetings that I think are essential you should have a weekly whip meeting a work in progress that just talks about what's coming up this week what have we got on what needs to happen What needs to get done where are we behind you should have a daily huddle which takes about 11 and 1/2 minutes to get through which just talks about what are we doing over the next 24 hours where are we stuck what are our numbers just a very simple update you should have a monthly strategy meeting with your leadership team to set the intention for the month you should have a quarterly strategy meeting with probably the whole team and you should have an annual offsite somewhere to talk about what you want to do over the next 12 months you should also have a one-on-one meeting every 14 days with your direct reports and I don't think you should have any more than five direct reports so if you've got more more than five people reporting to you it's time to elevate one of your team to a leadership role and have someone report to them because I don't think you can manage any more than five direct reports that's just my personal take you might differ that's totally fine but make sure you're having a one-on-one every 14 days with the people who report to you the advanced tactics and and how you know you're building a great team of a players is when your team Recruit new team members when your team take ownership of the team culture and when there are leadership Pathways developed so that your team can continue to evolve and develop personally and professionally I read a book a long time ago that said the three things that motivate employees are a cause they believe in a leader they respect and money in that order well I would add that more important than money and somewhere up with the cause they believe in and a leader they respect is a pathway where they can develop and evolve as a person because there's nothing worse than doing meaningless work that means I'm not evolving and I'm not growing as a person so make sure that you give your team opportunities and a pathway where they can see they can evolve and they can grow okay finally let's talk about the playbook for running your agency as an agency owner there is one standard operating procedure that you need to be across and that is the standard operating procedure for how to create standard operating procedures and you should train your team on how to create standard operating procedures the biggest mistake I see agency owners make when it comes to Sops and team is well I can't hire someone just yet because my processes aren't ready guess what your processors will never be ready to hire someone so hire people who know what they're doing and then get them to document the Sops way faster way to grow and it's not all on your head and by the way you are not the best person to document every part of the job and every part of the agency because you don't know how to do it all I mean you're not the best bookkeeper in the world you're not the best project manager and ad manager and social media manager and web developer and brand designer right and account manager you can't be all those things so hire people who are really good at what they do and then have them document the process to help you build up your playbook so train them on how to build the Sops and choose where those Sops are going to live format it train them how to do it and then incentivize your team to build those Sops for the agency Playbook as an agency owner you need to have a dashboard that tells you the health of your agency and CEO dashboards that we build for our agency owners show the revenue the recurring Revenue the profitability the growth the net gain in clients the churn the health score of the team and any other important metrics that the agency owner needs to see at a glance you will only be able to step away from your agency and take a break and tune out and actually unwind and disconnect from the agency if you have the peace of mind that the agency is running with without you and is not going to fall off the side of a cliff and the only way to do that is to actually look at the numbers in the business and make sure all the needles are moving in the right direction here's the problem with most agency owners who build a dashboard is they build a dashboard and then they spend their life trying to find the numbers to fill in the dashboard have your team responsible for filling in those numbers so that you can just review it every week and identify where the red lights and the yellow lights are we use the traffic light system here which says if we're on track to hit our monthly Target in any of our numbers it's a green light if we're at risk it's a yellow light if it's off track it's a red light and my job as the CEO is to make sure our team have everything they need to turn the red lights yellow and the yellow lights green I'm not responsible for changing the color of those lights the team are responsible my job is to make sure they have everything they need to succeed and the team's job is to update those numbers so that I can just look at it once a week and I know where we are most weeks there are yellow lights and there are some red lights that need changing that's just being in business but at least I've got visibility over the numbers and I know what to do to help the team move those dials in the right direction there's no way I could step away from this business with any kind of peace of mind if I didn't have the visibility over the most important numbers that tell me the health of the business and you know that your agency is documented and ultimately in a position where you can step away from it or even look to sell it when your master Playbook of all of your Sops is published and that could just be a workspace in notion that is updated every 90 days that your CEO dashboard is completely hands off meaning it gets updated every week - Alan Mullally who served as CEO of Ford Motor Company from 2006 to 2014 is often cited as a modern example of confident leadership when he took the helm Ford was in Dire Straits however with his Clear Vision and unwavering confidence malali led a successful turnaround he introduced the one Ford plan streamlining processes reducing costs and improving quality he had such confidence in the Ford brand that he revived one of their best-selling cars of all time which I'll reveal in a moment he sold off Jaguar Land Rover Aston Martin and Volvo and reduced their share in Mazda so they could focus on their core business selling Ford Motor Cars his confident decision making combined with open communication and accountability helped Ford become profitable again without taking a government bailout unlike all of its competitors in contrast Elizabeth Holmes founder and CEO of the now defunct Health technology company terenos showed signs of insecure leadership right from the start despite her career and vision the absence of transparency and accountability became defining characteristics of her leadership she made grandiose claims of course about teranos's technology that ultimately proved to be well as investigations began it was revealed that the company was using commercial analyzers for most tests instead of its proprietary technology this lack of transparency and defensiveness indicated a leadership style rooted in insecurity and deception which eventually led to the company's downfall and her imprisonment the good news is you get to choose confidence or insecurity more on that in a moment in other random facts from the interwebs mahoots who are elephant trainers typically fasten a thin metal chain around the leg of a fully grown elephant and secure the other end to a small wooden Peg driven into the ground despite the fact that the elephant stands at a towering height of 10 feet and weighs 10 000 pounds it could easily break the chain uproot the feeble wooden egg and escape to Freedom with minimal effort surprisingly though it never attempts to do so the world's Mightiest land animal capable of uprooting a substantial tree as effortlessly as snapping a toothpick remains captive to a small wooden Peg and a flimsy chain but why does this happen well as it turns out the elephant's trainers employed the same method to domesticate the poor thing when it was just a baby and during that time the chain and pig were actually strong enough to restrain the baby elephant whenever it tried to break free the metal chain would yank it back eventually the young elephant realized that escape was futile and stopped attempting to do so now even though the elephant has reached full maturity it still perceives the chain and the pig and recalls what it learned as a baby that they are insurmountable obstacles the the fact that this is no longer true the elephant's self-imposed limitations endure if we reflect on it we are all similar to elephants each of us possesses incredible power within ourselves along with our own metaphorical chains and pegs self-limiting thoughts and beliefs that hinder our progress now sometimes these are rooted in childhood experiences or past failures while other times they stem from things we were simply told when we were younger The crucial realization is this we must learn from the past while also being willing to challenge our assumptions and perspectives regarding the present life is not stagnant my friends and it is possible to break free from these constraints don't let your past butcher your future potential the good news is as I said you get to choose confidence or insecurity you get to choose what to believe the average human has over 70 000 thoughts a day that's a lot no wonder we're all exhausted most of them by the way are pointless a lot of them are actually quite disturbing and only a few will serve you on your journey so choose to believe the thoughts that are moving you forward instead of the ones that are holding you back I get a lot of feedback from people saying that they don't have the confidence to charge three five or ten thousand dollars a month whatever your number back in 1820 or whenever this was and uh so he settled that debt with 400 head of cattle and sam being a lawyer and not a rancher didn't really know what to do with these 400 head of cattle so he didn't have a fence he didn't have any fencing on his acreage and he didn't have a brand to brand them so the cattle just kind of went where they wanted to go and they did what they wanted to do and people to leave in for you to fill up your workshops you can use this exact same strategy for webinars for strategy sessions literally anything that you might have where you can help somebody it sounds so simple most people won't do it but I'm telling you it'll absolutely Crush for you the third strategy and it might be the easiest one is to design a special offer for the holidays so Global sales rise 10% in December and this is your chance to take advantage of that consumer Behavior there's three types of offers a limited time offer a discount offer and a freebie offer you can can leverage one two or even all three to capitalize on this buying window the limited time offer is an offer that expires after a certain period of time the discount offer is providing a reduction on starts hey name I'm putting a very special offer together from now until the end of the year I'm offering a and then insert your discount on your service or product plus if you buy within the next 48 - about 10 years ago I was visiting San Francisco and I was going to a square and Google and all these other tech companies and as I walked around their offices I noticed that they pretty much create an environment where all they do is work because everything else is taken care of for them they have the clean and full for the laundry they have the daycare they have the canteens they have the snack drawers they have everything around them so that it takes care of all that extra stuff that you normally have to think about so that they could do the thing that creates the most value for the business and when I brought that back home to my wife I was like you know we both work from home and we could probably use some support in our home to be able to like Buy Back Time sure we had a cleaning person sure we had somebody helping us with meal prep but to take all of that and consolidate it since we both had offices in our home I was like wow this could be a really cool idea and I went down the rabbit hole and I found out that there's a role called the house manager it takes care of anything in your home that would otherwise be you or your partner so it frees up your time to do the thing in your business that makes you the most money and or have the time for yourself to recharge or spend it with the people you love the most like your kids and your family so I want to walk through exactly how we found hired trained and have supported our life through our house manager to help you buy back your time the first strategy is we want to talk about consolidating the roles to reduce complexity see a lot of people they might already have somebody that you know runs errands for them or somebody's separate that comes to their house and cleans or somebody else that helps with meal prep and they feel like oh this is really efficient but the challenge is is that you now have four or five people that you've got to coordinate My Philosophy is to consolidate when we started doing this you know we actually asked our cleaning lady Shannon hey could you stop by the grocery store once a week and get kind of the core stable of stuff and then eventually went from you know groceries to meal prep to you know even babysitting our kids since they were already in the home why not ask them to support you at a higher level you know one day I was at my friend's house and he was complaining that his wife wasn't on board to have somebody kind of come in and be around more and I was like what's the thing that you want off your plate and he's like well honestly it'd be nice if I didn't have to go wash my cars every week and put gas in them and you know do all the kind of the maintenance around that and I was like well you have a cleaning lady and he's like yeah and I said why don't you ask them to support you he's like whoa I never thought about that it's like at the end of the day there's probably somebody in your life that you trust that you already pay that you could pay the same amount to do other stuff for you like run errands and go shopping and get groceries for you or support the meal prepper whatever it is to buy back your time so that you can do something that you love take care of yourself and or invest in your business so that you can grow your business to have more resources the second strategy is to have one person that's the true owner that creates proactive results see when you consolidate all these different roles into one person then you can delegate the outcome of managing the stuff in your life so for example my executive assistant they manage all the corporate stuff all the emails and the legals and the financials for all my different companies that I'm involved in my schedule my time my travel and then everything that's on the house manager side is anything that's personal which I call personal ink they are the CEO of the cars and the Assets in the real estate and the registrations and the insurance and all of the little things that when you add them all together can take 10 to 20 30 40 hours a week of extra time that you don't even realize and when you have two busy entrepreneurs in a family having somebody like that is indispensable I can't even explain how valuable that would be to your life and for me the biggest tip I would give you is to have your house manager report to your executive assistant the reason why is because they know your calendar they know your schedule they know where you're going to be and if you want somebody that's in your home to be able to prep things pack for things go purchase stuff run errands they can work with that person that's around you to do that so being in your office and having documents brought to you sign them brought to the the law firm having all your mail processing and then checks being brought to your bank and moving Parcels around and shipping gifts to people and all that stuff having it coordinated through your executive assistant with your house manager is a game changer to buy back your time to have the focus to do the thing that only you can do the third strategy that I want to share with you is how to find this person so you avoid hiring a dud you know most people when I share this they're like okay how do I find this person where did you find yours I don't understand that sounds like such a crazy thing like that sounds that's a big role to hire for and My Philosophy is very simple if other people in their life manage their life why can't they manage my stuff why can't they come in and coordinate and organize and clean and purchase and and schedule stuff in my life they're doing it for themselves so my whole philosophy has always been what I call the test first hiring method it really comes down to three parts number one I want to use a video submission on the application to quickly vet and see if somebody can follow instructions it's very simple okay number two is I'm going to use a Profile assessment one of my favorites is called the Colby a and it tells me if somebody has a high follow through are they somebody that is a good researcher people that essentially compliment me the opposite of me if I hire somebody that's going to help support me I don't want to hire another me I want to hire the opposite so they can take things off of my plate the third thing we want to do is a test project and when it comes to a house manager my favorite is to actually simulate what I'm going to expect them to do so I might ask them to deal with some mail go make a purchase babysit my kids whatever you're going to ask your house manager do you want to simulate that and just see how did they show up what questions did they ask did you give them all the information did they read the play did you gel with their their behavior their attitude their Vibe because you're going to have them in your home if they didn't connect with you and your partner and and your kids then that's not somebody you want to move forward you want to find somebody that you feel at ease with in your space but also can take things off your plate and make you feel comfortable number number four is all about task and management you want to avoid under performance meaning that what is the process you're going to use to manage them so for me I'm a big fan of weekly one-on-one you know some people make the mistake when they have somebody come and support them in their home to treat them like a friend and be very loosey-goosey with their expectations and you know always want to be polite and talk to them et cetera I mean the way I look at it is they have work to do they have outcomes to achieve and I want to stay out of their way I don't want to interrupt them and Bug them and when I do that I use the one-on-one weekly meeting to get a line to get feedback on my my direction am I being clear to understand if they're bottlenecked are they overwhelm what's missing in our relationship and then some tips is to use software to manage the communication the project management and the account in detail so to manage all the passwords and codes that might come in all of our real estate and our lives we use one password which is a great software for sharing that stuff we use Google Docs demand manage the systems and the playbooks that they're creating on an ongoing process to manage the people and the things and the the timeline for stuff and then we use Trello because trello's an easy project management software to just know what are you working on this week what's next week what's in the queue to eventually discuss and scope out and give you the resources to complete some other bigger projects to really support the family you know I want you to consider this quote by Jim Rohn that time is more valuable than money because you can get more money but you can't get more time so if you've been on the fence of bringing in a house manager in your life I hope this acts as a sign to get you to finally make the hire bring them in train them manage them to buy back that time to take your life to another level so if you found this video useful be sure to leave a comment below let me know what resonated the most with you what did you take away what did you need to hear most and as per usual I want to challenge you to live a bigger life and a bigger business I'll see you next Monday foreign

- here's five secrets for building software even if you don't know how to code you know one of my buddies Taylor came to me one day and he's like hey I want to get into this SAS world you know teach ideas from your customers and put together a package where you can actually get them to fund your development and even better use that package to hire the team and make sure that you don't over build that is the secret so I walked them through the process and I shared with you guys today and it allowed him to go within six months from zero to having a working product in the market and generating 350 000 in sales for their software this is how we do it these are the five Secrets let's get into it the first secret is to find a pain problem find a problem that the market has your customers have that they're willing to pay for you know my first literally I've never shared this before but the thing that I built that I charge for the first product that I ever created was a CD burning app I was literally 18 years old I had within the first CD ROM burners on my computer and all my friends would come over because we were downloading songs off of Napster and they would make these CDs for their girlfriends and I was like sitting there watching them on my computer day after day hour over hour just like obsessing over the 5 000 songs I downloaded over the internet to make their perfect 152 song playlist and it would just get to me because they were using my computer I want to be on there gaming and chatting So eventually I sat down and I coded this software in Visual Basic that allowed them to install on their home computers uh connection to my computer to download all the songs I had so they could make their own CD ease and then order it click a button it would send it over to my computer and at night I would put a CD in the burner and it would burn the songs for them to get it was 20 bucks that was the first thing I ever built that somebody paid for and inside of me something kind of snapped I was like oh this is this is crazy I built this thing people pay me for it and all of a sudden I kind of felt like oh there's more to this this software world where I could like build other tools and maybe have people pay for it and that to me is the filter number one for deciding what idea should I pursue so many people build things that oftentimes turn into vitamins right These are nice to haves they're not must-haves we want to build painkillers so the tip that I want to share with you is that if you're trying to find ideas that people are currently paying for go to Consultants go to custom Dev shops ask them if you're trying to build an AI software for logistic companies find the development agency the people that build software four logistic companies and go see what are the things they're currently building for customers what's the custom Integrations what's the actual dollars they're spending money on what's the actual problems that they're paying people to build code to solve those problems to help you understand what would the first list of features be that I want to build for my new software second secret is we want to prototype the product what this means is we want to create something that we can actually show a potential buyer so they give us feedback before we go and spend a bunch of money on getting custom code built you know the first thing that I ever kind of helped somebody bill was my brother was looking to build a software for managing his properties he had a bunch of tenants and he wanted to build a product that would allow them to like go online and request issues being fixed in their apartments or whatever it is so all I did is I took a clipboard with paper Okay white printer paper a black Sharpie and Drew different screens and different flows to demonstrate to a potential user how it would work and then to get feedback I went to the mall the local mall super not fancy this is 20 years ago and just stop people and ask them like do you live in an apartment to be like yeah why and I'd be like well I'm thinking of building this software it's called manage Street and I'm just curious like when I show you this what would you do with that screen and they're like what are you talking talking about and I was like well my brother's taking a building this software and I'd love your feedback because the last thing he wants to do is build something and do it in a way that you might not actually find it useful and I sat there and I would just talk to people in the mall just randomly to get feedback on the product and we learned so much we learned about the things that were important to them what they expected the first time user experience to look like what would happen after a button what were the screens that would pop up and we just write down these notes to help us get feedback the key is is that we want to build a clickable prototype that is the concept in today's world we have software like balsamic or figma or Envision app that creates near Pixel Perfect software with no code you will save all your time all your money by doing that first so that you then have the specs and get feedback from real customers that could buy it so that you don't go and build a bunch of stuff that nobody's going to find useful the Third Secret is we want to pre-sell to fund what this means is we want to take the clickable prototype this this design of the first spec of our software and actually go put it in front of potential buyers and take their money some of you guys may think like that's crazy why would people pay for software here's the deal my whole thing is that if somebody's not willing to pay you for that software before it's ever built then you may be building something that doesn't really have a problem that they care about if you think about crowdfunding these are all examples of where people see a thing get excited about the problem it's going to solve in their life and prepay and buy into it one of my companies flowtown which was a marketing tool for small businesses to get the social media data on their customers through email addresses before we ever built the full product we wanted to test it we wanted to get early customers to pay for it so what we did is we created a landing page put the three features and the benefits that you would get from using this software even when as far as having a sign why not process an upload screen for the data that they were giving us showed them what it would cost to use the software collected a credit card and when they click submit we didn't even charge the credit card and we made up this fake excuse that our servers were overloaded and that we'd get back to them when the product was available I mean we wanted to validate you know so by sending hundreds of emails having people go through the flow upload the data and click the buy button so that we had real validated customers that were getting software that changed the game for us you know I had another friend of mine Ryan he built this software called bucket and I walked him through the same process and he built the clickable prototype launched the program he called it the Inner Circle program and he had a bunch of people hundreds of people fund the development of his software by getting them to buy into the vision that they were building so that he didn't have to spend a lot of money building stuff that nobody wanted because he just showed them here's what I'm going to go build this is when you're going to get it and the way to do it and I call this the early adopter program is you want them to prepay for a year but give them a 50 discount so if you're going to do an inner circle or what I call an early adopter program you want to give them whatever the annual cost would be give them a 50 discount to get early access to use your software to co-create it with you to even have their name this is what Ryan did he put their name on his website to be these are the early supporters of our product so that they felt like they were part of a movement you have to get people to pay ahead of time to truly validate if you've got something worth building the fourth secret is we want to test the team see most people make the mistake of like wiring 50 100 200 Grand to a development agency and saying here's this thing please build it see the benefit of having a clickable prototype is really simple now I have the product specs I have something that is defining what I'm asking you to build so people can give you a really clear estimate and then you can take one of those specific features and Dole it out to to two or three different developers and ask them to build it as part of a test project when I was building my company Clarity which was a Marketplace for entrepreneurs to get advice over the phone I had a bunch of early customers put deposits to show me they were serious I then took the spec went on upwork a freelancing site for developers to build different prototypes and I could see who communicated better who wrote the best code who built the thing on time and then that gave me the sense of relief to hire the right person to build the product and what's cool is because I had the Prototype I had all of the specs because one of the key tips is you never want a developer to design your software it's called developer design it's the biggest mistake I can see it almost every time I come across software somebody's like hey look I got this thing built and I'm like you let a developer choose the labels on the buttons and the screens and the flows and it's complicated when you have a clickable prototype it allows you to test first different people find the right person and then give them the full project to build out the fifth secret is customer backwards see most people make the mistake of when they launch software they put it out in the world they built something it's their baby and people start signing up for it and they're just so hungry to like learn from people so they're asking for advice and the truth is is everybody's got an opinion and they'll give it to you but what I've discovered is you only want to get feedback from people that are using the product you know when I was building my company Clarity I had a bunch of people say to me great product I love the idea congratulations and I'd be like oh great you have any thoughts any feedback any any advice for me and they'd be like oh yeah you know you should do this you should consider that you know next time you should launch with this integration and then I remember one time I was getting this feedback this person was super opinionated and then I went on the back end admin system to like check out their account and I noticed that they never actually use the product sure they had an account they signed up for it but they never actually went through and did anything with the software and it made me realize that if I'm going to spend time money in improving something I have to only talk to people that are actually actively using the product so it's customer backwards I need people that are paying and using and getting their feedback so one of the tips is this great survey called the customer development survey and there's a bunch of different aspects that I think will really serve you so you can go Google it check it out online customer development survey it's pretty well known in the product world but the key there is when you're getting feedback from the customers you want to segment from people that are not active you do not want to take feedback from people that are not active because the ones that are the ones that would be very disappointed if the product went away they answer the question of hey what's one thing we could change to better meet your needs in our product and the people that are active in the product that answer that question those are the folks that you want to Value their feedback everybody else is just noise and it can absolutely send you down on a rabbit Chase so if you're thinking of building software be sure to follow this process I know it's exciting I want to move ahead real quick and build something and it's so much fun and we can do no harm and wouldn't this idea be creative and so cool and then you're gonna call me one day when you've spent tens maybe hundreds of thousands of dollars on an outsourced Dev team that you built the product that you finally launched that nobody's using and that's unfortunately the call I get so if you found this useful be sure to leave a comment I want to invite you to join my scaling SAS Founders Facebook group it's a free group where we connect with other Founders just like you that are building software that don't know how to code and we're sharing resources and best practices and strategies to grow their business and I want to leave you with this thought that the best type of financing is from a customer okay customer financing is the best form of financing with that I want to challenge you to live a bigger life and a bigger business and I'll see you next Monday foreign
  — *danmartell: How to Start a SaaS with ZERO Coding Skills*
